item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Neither of the images that were used in this article could be sourced, and thus are assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually found the money test log amusing. I do enjoy it when SCP articles remind us that the Foundation personnel are actual human beings with ulterior motives and questionable judgement, and the money experiment is a succinct and entertaining way of doing that. I don't understand the people who accuse those parts of being too "lolfoundation". Lolfoundation is more for hardcore crack fics that twist the concept on its ear, and I don't get that impression. This is an altogether whimsical SCP with the potential for beauty, humor, and horror, and I respect that.

On that note, I would give anything to have the glass tree and the cheese tree.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Michael Rosen once ate a bag of plums.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta love how they skipped implanting a seed in a live cow and instead went straight from hamburger to using a D-class. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really needs an extended testing log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually just saw a comic over at Scandinavia and the World about that hoax! It's amazing the things that people will believe. :P

It looks like the original author, Ink Asylum, hasn't been active in over a year though, so I don't think a new addition is likely.quack

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has got to be one of my favourite SCPs. I love the concept.

I got reminded of it when I was watching a short video on the history of April Fool's Day and some of the best things to come out of it - including Panorama's trick from 1957 about growing spaghetti trees…

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spaghetti-tree_hoax

I've no idea if the original author is still around, but I wonder how silly a reference to Panorama's hoax would be to include. Pasta 1147!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion, this idea is fantastic. I love how this is written as well. +1

item.title: Dang...
item.content:encoded: I go away for a few weeks, and come back to this. Now I'm both creeped out and simultaneously craving plums. +1 for good storytelling!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really do like the idea but in some places the tone seems extremely un-scientific, for example

  • I would recommend disciplinary action against Researcher Adams for liberally sampling SCP-1147-BE-2 before proper D-Class testing, but I believe the hangover is punishment enough. -Dr. Laure
  • I told you it wouldn't work. I'm glad I didn't chip in to the collection. -Dr. Laurel
  • Resembles a plush plum. Edible, but just barely

Just pointing it out

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, this one is gorgeous. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bit of fridge horror here - this thing would be a Godsend to GoIs that make\use SCPs, and a possible nightmare for the foundation if accidental cross-contamination occurred in a containment facility - or even worse, cross-contamination by an uncontained instance of SCp-1147 in the wild.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having been away from the site for a while I was excited to see my very first article break 100, but confused when seeing test cases I don't remember writing. Checking the history I saw that they were added by someone else and have since removed them as I felt they detracted from my original article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was a fun read, and I even liked most of the lolFoundation notes (didn't care for the marijuana note).

Also:

Fruit: Dark yellow with a honeycomb-like texture. When the first specimen was bitten into by a test subject, the fruit was discovered to be filled with bees. Test subject was quickly treated for anaphylactic shock. After removing the bees, the fruit was found to be sweet and sticky to the touch.

Fruit was bees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good，but the Test Logs might be too many…I mean,I even get a little bit annoyed when scrolling down the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good，but the Test Logs might be too many…I mean,I even get a little bit annoyed when scrolling down the page.
Congratulations! What will you do now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's why it would be confiscated, a golden plum tree with golden fruit surely would cause some degree of commotion among the staff :D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, I'm pretty sure it would just be gold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about plant it in a █ kg gold bullion?

Although I'm pretty sure the result and fruit would be [DATA EXPUNGED] and the specimen will be confiscated by O5's command.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just saw the new addition of SCP-1147-GS. Isn't "demotion to D-class" no longer acceptable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plant it in an SCP-500 solution, I wonder what would happen.

item.title: Friendly tip,
item.content:encoded: OK. Got it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just going to give you a friendly tip, posting on various SCPs asking what would happen if you crosstested them with famous SCPs is a good way to make people dislike you really fast. This goes double for 682 and 173.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if you put a seed on SCP-173? Would the SCP-173 hybrid plum tree be chasing after people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent and very creative. I initially feared it was going to be too similar to 914, but you showed me wrong. The comical notes didn't bother me that much, though you could replace Dr Laurel's name with a black box, since knowing who he is doesn't add anything to the article. In fact, I laughed a lot when I saw that they were literally trying to grow money on trees and it didn't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one a lot, it gives off a theme of nature trying to survive like humans-
-adapt anywhere, everywhere. Also, i'm having a great time thinking of what happened with SCP-1147-BR, and i'm a bit scared now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one.
As I was reading through the test logs, I was fascinated by the creativity put into this.
Then I got to the part where they started testing it on humans. I don't know why, but this scares the hell out of me. Just imagine being strapped down and having a seed planted in your arm, then growing an additional limb and dying on the operating table for some unknown reason. Or regaining sapience afterwards because a seed was planted in your brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a fan! Love the execution and the concept, it's lighthearted but still in keeping with the site theme,, and the lolfoundation-ness isn't overused or unfunny.

+1, my good man. Perhaps a testing log could be in order to expand on it, and maybe cut down on a Things Wot Kill You side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They weren't missed. Cross-SCP testing should only be done if it adds anything to the effectiveness of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Derp doublepost

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised that there were a few experiments that were missed, like trying to grow it in a medium of mixed materials, or even material SCPs that could be useful but are in limited amounts, like say: Telekill, Bladewood or SCP500

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idea: You should drop a seed in molten metal or burning napalm and see if it grows fire flowers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done. This is a good example of a 914-like Safe SCP article; interesting without necessarily being dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent Safe SCP, a great bit of pacing and tonal palate cleanser for anyone moving through the list or clicking random. It helps the naturalism of the entire Foundation if they aren't constantly sacrificing Ds and rolling out mobile task forces to stop OMEGA KETER. If anything, I think the death implied in the last log felt gratuitous and unnecessary - like a spasm in the usual 'everything's deadly, everything's waiting to kill you!' direction. I'd cut it.

This won't be getting a lot of 'my favorite SCP!' links, but with any justice, it'll be a go-to lesson on how to put together a clean, interesting, well-executed concept without endless arbitrary mazes or an insatiable appetite for Ds.

Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Were the tests predictable once I knew what was going on? Yes. Was there a bit of lolfoundation? Yes. Did it fail the "potentially useful, I want one now" test? Oh goodness yes.

Did it do all that in such a way as to make it a *better* entry? Paradoxically, yes! Maybe the fact that you wrote this very well (prior gripes about individual things notwithstanding since I'm only seeing it in the way it is at the time of me writing this) helped, but it's a very good addition. And personally I just love a good Safe object that - except in a very few, unlikely situations - really poses no great harm, but is being contained just because of how weird it is.

Also, thank you, "Water plums" and "Ale fruits" are going to be showing up in my next D&D campaign when I blackmail convince everyone to play Dark Sun…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the comments. I'll make those changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the water tree I was trying to describe a tree that looked like ice with a water core, but wasn't cold. I'll see if I can make that more clear.

I'll take a look at the steel entry, too. It's a bit unclear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There have been some really good first SCPs around here lately. Thank you for the good read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
some things that jumped out at me:

The original specimen of SCP-1147 was discovered growing from the steel base of a lamp post in ████ ██████, Georgia, where it was suspected to be an art installation by the local population.

suspected to be? believed to be.

Substance: 20cm cube of stainless steel.
Notes: This is the same material in which SCP-1147 was discovered. Original specimen successfully reproduced. -Dr. Laurel

aren't lamp posts usually galvanized mild steel, not stainless? (actually, they're usually fiberglass, but that's neither here nore there…)

Substance: Raw coal ground into rough powder.

the listed results would make more sense if this was charcoal, rather than coal.

Fruit: Edible, but largely flavorless. D-431 received mild cuts on lips/tongue.

"minor" would be much more clinical than "mild".

separately: i like the idea, but i'm downvoting for pretty much all of the notes from dr laurel. the article can stand on its own without rimshots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this. It is well written and even the lolfoundation jokes were good. One little issue for me is that it takes about 4 years in optimal conditions for a plum tree to bear fruit, which means a lot of these experiments must have taken place at the same time. Also, much of the town must be blind of it took that long for them to notice the tree growing from the lamp post. Still, minor issues. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's pliable, then it likely didn't - high carbon steel would break.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. If I'm reading this article, I'm going to be wondering what happens with some of the more lolfoundationy entries, and it's nice to have an article that addresses them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. I actually like the lolfoundation here, for whatever reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I think that the trees should be even more like the "soil" than currently. For example, the seeds "planted" in filtered water shouldn't produce a tree with a very high water content, it should be water, which maintains the tree shape in some anomalous manner.

Also, for the animal related tests, it might be interesting to have one where the "soil" is a slurry composed from a colony of social insects like ants, to see if it develops into a lot of tiny trees that cooperate, or a single tree with detachable leaves that go out and do things for the "queen" trunk, or something.

Trunk is high quality steel with a superior tensile strength. Leaves and flowers are thin, pliable steel sheets and give off no scent.

So did it improve on the original steel by adding carbon to it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this enough for an upvote. Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely do expand the -BR one, if you have what with to. At the mment, there isn't that much there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, just great. I want one. My only suggestion is the last note, in the brain test, is a bit too lolfoundation and breaks tone. I did enjoy the others though, especially the beer one.

item.title: Re: Less cheeky staff shenanigans
item.content:encoded: Damn. You're right. I'll fix it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The animalia test log said "close liquid test log" when you opened it. I fixed that.

item.title: Re: Less cheeky staff shenanigans
item.content:encoded: Maybe it would be better redacted? I'm tempted to expound on SCP-1147-BR more, but don't want to bloat the article. Maybe I'll come back and add a sub-page if this gets more popular.

item.title: Re: Less cheeky staff shenanigans
item.content:encoded:
Meh.
It's lolfoundationy, but funny lolfoundation, not boring shit.

That said… botany is a subset of biology - plant biology. Makes Dr. Laurel sound like an idiot, and will probably get a miffed Photo on your ass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to worry. :) I know the benefits of moderation.

item.title: Re: Less cheeky staff shenanigans
item.content:encoded:
With that, this article enters upvote territory for me. I still feel like the beer test is a bit lolfoundation-y, but the article is good enough to carry on past it.

I do find it odd that the results of a Foundation experiment would be expunged instead of redacted, though. That seems like information we might need access to if we encounter more specimens of SCP-1147 in the field, say, at a graveyard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^This. As said, I love humor, and I would've been a bit more conservative with an upvote were there none here. But I've seen a few newbie articles get shot down due to lolfoundation, and I didn't want that to happen here. But it looks like it's not.
Ink, just don't add a wry comment after every single freaking test, and you'll be fine.

item.title: Re: Less cheeky staff shenanigans
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I think I went a step too far. Removing SCP-1147-FE, so there's just one beer joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There needs to be a scenario in which a seed falls into a researcher's pocket and grows into a pants-tree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather uncreative concept, saved by interesting execution. First SCP I've read all the way through in a while.
+1

item.title: Less cheeky staff shenanigans
item.content:encoded:
The test logs make the article for me, but there's too much "X got drunk lol" staff shenanigans for me to upvote. A lot of what you have here is good, but the comments on things like the beer and feces tests really detract from what is otherwise a very solid article.

I'd upvote if that was cleaned up though.
